text: Publisher’s description The policing of rape is in permacrisis. This book interrogates and addresses the complex of questions that arise when considering why police investigations of rape fail to serve victim-survivors on anything other than a tiny minority of occasions, and offers a framework for thinking about what police can do in order to change this. Low conviction rates and poor victim-survivor experiences are at the heart of the rape justice gap. Thirty years of research, policy and law reform has made some changes to how rape investigations are carried out. These changes have alleviated some of the ‘ills’ of current procedures, but for victim-survivors the investigation of rape is still a highly unpleasant and traumatic experience - for the vast majority of complainants, that is. Police forces are intrinsically resistant to reform, and are especially so when reform is required to address crimes in which public trust is low and likely to remain low, such as rape. So if we want radical change, we have to do things differently. This book begins by interrogating the question of why rape is an especially difficult crime to have investigated properly, but moves on to the question of why it is also therefore difficult for the police themselves to investigate rape properly. Policing Rape offers a practice-based theory for effecting transformative and lasting change to the investigation of rape, as well as to the prevention of rape itself, and to officers' engagement with victim-survivors. It draws on the authors' experience and reflections of their role within Operation Soteria Bluestone, a pioneering project designed to effect significant and enduring change in rape investigations and beyond. Policing Rape will be of huge interest to scholars and practitioners working in the field of rape and sexual violence, policing, criminology, victimology and law.
explanation: The text reads like a polished publisher's description with coherent argument and domain-specific references (e.g., Operation Soteria Bluestone). It lacks typical AI-generation telltales such as repetitive phrasing or abrupt topic shifts, making human authorship more likely.
